schools.summary: Gill: the word for the offerer is 'a soul,' that is, a man, and the meat offering, or rather meal or bread offering, was of fine flour, with oil and frankincense; the witnesses note God appointing the meat offering to be of fine flour, with oil poured on and frankincense laid on it — the appointing of the meal-offering, of flour, oil, and frankincense, a thank-offering of a sweet savour (the offering of the finest flour, with oil and incense: 'his offering shall be of fine flour; and he shall pour oil upon it, and put frankincense thereon'; the matter, 'of fine flour,' not flesh, but the finest of the wheat, finely ground, the fruit of the earth and of man's labour; the oil, 'pour oil upon it,' the oil mingled with the flour, the type of the Holy Spirit; the frankincense, 'put frankincense thereon,' the sweet spice burnt with it, the type of the sweet savour of grace and of acceptable devotion; the type the commentators draw, that the meat offering, of fine flour mingled with oil and frankincense, shadows Christ in his perfect humanity, the fine flour, evenly ground, without unevenness or sin, full of the Spirit, the oil, and the sweet savour of his graces, the frankincense, offered to God; that this was a thank- and dedication-offering, not for sin, the fruit of the ground given to God).

debates.question: What does the meat offering of fine flour, oil, and frankincense shadow?

sides.position: Christ in his perfect humanity — the fine flour, evenly ground, without unevenness or sin — full of the Holy Spirit (the oil), and the sweet savour of his graces and devotion (the frankincense), offered to God; a thank- and dedication-offering, not for sin

themes: his offering shall be of fine flour
and he shall pour oil upon it, and put frankincense thereon
the meat offering of fine flour, with oil (the Spirit) and frankincense (the sweet savour of grace)
a thank- and dedication-offering shadowing Christ in his perfect humanity, full of the Spirit, the sweet savour of his graces

schools.summary: Gill: the offerer brought it to the priest and left it with him, who took out a handful of the flour and oil, with all the frankincense, and burnt it on the altar for a memorial; the witnesses note the offerer bringing the meat offering to the priests, who took a handful of the flour and oil with all the frankincense and burnt it for a memorial, a sweet savour to the LORD — the burning of a handful with all the incense, the part for the whole, a sweet savour (a memorial-handful burnt to God: 'he shall take thereout his handful of the flour... and of the oil... with all the frankincense... and the priest shall burn the memorial of it upon the altar... of a sweet savour unto the LORD'; the bringing, 'bring it to Aaron's sons the priests,' that the offerer left it with the priest, who offered it; the handful, 'his handful of the flour... and of the oil,' a token portion, the part standing for the whole; that all the frankincense was burnt, the whole of the sweet incense given to God; the burning, 'burn the memorial of it upon the altar,' a memorial, a part offered to put God in remembrance, the rest given to the priests; the acceptance, 'of a sweet savour unto the LORD,' that the offering was well-pleasing; the type, that the sweet savour of Christ's graces, the frankincense, all given to God, and his devotion ascended to God, a memorial accepted).

themes: he shall take thereout his handful of the flour... and of the oil... with all the frankincense... and the priest shall burn the memorial of it upon the altar
of a sweet savour unto the LORD
a handful of the flour and oil with all the frankincense burnt for a memorial, a sweet savour
the part standing for the whole, all the sweet incense given to God; the sweet savour of Christ's graces and devotion ascending, accepted

schools.summary: Gill: the remnant of the meat offering was Aaron's and his sons', which shows the care taken by the Lord for the maintenance of his priests, and it was a thing most holy; the witnesses note God giving the remnant of the meat offering, after the memorial was burnt, to Aaron and his sons, a thing most holy — the priests' portion of the offering, God's provision for his ministers (the rest the priests', and it most holy: 'the remnant of the meat offering shall be Aaron's and his sons': it is a thing most holy'; the portion, 'the remnant... shall be Aaron's and his sons',' that, after the handful was burnt to God, the rest was given to the priests for food, the care taken by the Lord for the maintenance of his priests; the holiness, 'it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire,' that this portion was most holy, to be eaten only by the priests, in the holy place; the meaning the commentators draw, that God provides for those who serve at his altar, they which wait at the altar are partakers with the altar, and that the holy things are for the holy; the type, that they who feed on Christ, the meat offering, must be the consecrated, the priests of God; that God's ministers are fed from the offerings).

themes: And the remnant of the meat offering shall be Aaron's and his sons'
it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire
the remnant of the meat offering given to the priests for food, a thing most holy
God's provision for the maintenance of his priests; they which wait at the altar partakers with the altar, the holy things for the holy

schools.summary: Gill: this is another kind of meat offering, or in another form, baked in the oven, of unleavened cakes of fine flour mingled with oil, or unleavened wafers anointed with oil; the witnesses note God appointing the meat offering baked in the oven to be unleavened cakes of fine flour mingled with oil, or unleavened wafers anointed with oil — the dressed meal-offering, baked, yet still of fine flour and oil and unleavened (the offering baked, but still pure and oiled: 'an oblation of a meat offering baken in the oven, it shall be unleavened cakes of fine flour mingled with oil, or unleavened wafers anointed with oil'; the form, 'baken in the oven,' that the offering might be brought, not as raw flour, but as cakes or wafers, dressed by fire; the cakes, 'unleavened cakes of fine flour mingled with oil,' the thicker cakes, with the oil mingled in; the wafers, 'unleavened wafers anointed with oil,' the thinner wafers, with the oil spread on; the constant marks, that all was still of fine flour, unleavened, and with oil, the same purity, freedom from leaven, and anointing of the Spirit; the type the commentators draw, that the various forms, raw, oven-baked, pan-baked, shadow the various sufferings and trials of Christ, dressed by the fire of affliction, yet ever pure, without the leaven of sin, and full of the Spirit; that, however dressed, the offering is pure and oiled).

themes: an oblation of a meat offering baken in the oven, it shall be unleavened cakes of fine flour mingled with oil
or unleavened wafers anointed with oil
the meat offering baked into unleavened cakes or wafers, still of fine flour and oil
the various forms shadowing the various sufferings of Christ, dressed by the fire of affliction, yet ever pure and full of the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: the pan had no edge or covering, and the paste on it was hard, that it might be parted in pieces; the offering was of fine flour, unleavened, mingled with oil; the witnesses note God appointing the meat offering baked on a pan to be of fine flour, unleavened, mingled with oil — another form of the dressed offering, still pure and oiled (the pan-baked offering too of fine flour and oil: 'if thy oblation be a meat offering baken in a pan, it shall be of fine flour unleavened, mingled with oil'; the form, 'baken in a pan,' on a flat pan without edge or covering, the paste baked hard; the constant marks, 'of fine flour unleavened, mingled with oil,' the same fine flour, the same freedom from leaven, the same oil mingled, as in the other forms; that, however the offering was dressed, the essentials were unchanged, purity, fine flour, no corruption, unleavened, and the Spirit, oil; the type before given, that the differing forms shadow the differing ways and trials in which Christ was offered, yet ever the same, pure and full of the Spirit; that God will have his offerings, in whatever form, pure and oiled).

themes: if thy oblation be a meat offering baken in a pan
it shall be of fine flour unleavened, mingled with oil
the meat offering baked on a pan, still of fine flour, unleavened, and mingled with oil
the essentials unchanged in every form: purity, no corruption, and the Spirit; a shadow of Christ ever the same, pure and full of the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: this answered to the dividing of the pieces of the burnt offering, and signified the same thing; the oil was poured on it; the witnesses note God appointing the baked offering to be parted in pieces and oil poured on it, for it is a meat offering — the breaking of the offering and the pouring on of oil (the offering divided and freshly oiled: 'Thou shalt part it in pieces, and pour oil thereon: it is a meat offering'; the parting, 'part it in pieces,' that the baked cake was broken into pieces, which answered to the dividing of the pieces of the burnt offering, and signified the same thing; the oil, 'pour oil thereon,' that, even after baking, fresh oil was poured on, the offering never without the oil; the meaning the commentators draw, that the breaking in pieces may shadow the breaking of Christ's body in his sufferings, and the abundant oil the fulness of the Spirit poured upon him without measure; that the offering, broken, was yet full of the oil, the Spirit not lacking; that what is offered to God is broken and yet anointed).

themes: Thou shalt part it in pieces, and pour oil thereon
it is a meat offering
the baked offering parted in pieces and fresh oil poured on it
some seeing the breaking shadow the breaking of Christ's body, and the abundant oil the fulness of the Spirit poured on him without measure

schools.summary: Gill: the difference between the pan and the frying pan: the frying pan has a cover and is deep, the pan has no cover; the offering was made of fine flour with oil; the witnesses note God appointing the meat offering baked in the frying pan to be of fine flour with oil — yet another form of the dressed offering, still pure and oiled (the frying-pan offering too of fine flour and oil: 'if thy oblation be a meat offering baken in the fryingpan, it shall be made of fine flour with oil'; the form, 'baken in the fryingpan,' a deep, covered pan, distinct from the open pan; the constant marks, 'of fine flour with oil,' the same fine flour and the same oil, in this as in the other forms; that, in every manner of dressing, raw, oven, pan, frying pan, the offering kept the same essentials, the fine flour of purity and the oil of the Spirit; the type before given, that the differing forms shadow the differing ways and trials in which Christ was offered, yet ever the same, pure and full of the Spirit; that God allows variety in the manner, but requires the same purity and anointing).

themes: if thy oblation be a meat offering baken in the fryingpan
it shall be made of fine flour with oil
the meat offering baked in the frying pan, still of fine flour with oil
the same essentials in every form: the fine flour of purity and the oil of the Spirit; a shadow of Christ ever the same

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the meat offering made of these things was to be brought unto the LORD, presented to the priest, who brought it to the altar; the witnesses note the offerer bringing the meat offering to the LORD, presenting it to the priest, who brought it to the altar — the presenting of the offering through the priest, the way of approach (the offering brought to God by the priest's hand: 'thou shalt bring the meat offering... unto the LORD: and when it is presented unto the priest, he shall bring it unto the altar'; the bringing, 'bring the meat offering... unto the LORD,' to the tabernacle, the house of the LORD, and to the LORD's priest; the presenting, 'when it is presented unto the priest, he shall bring it unto the altar,' that the offerer brought it, but the priest presented it at the altar; the order, that the offering came to God not directly but through the priest, who alone might bring it to the altar; the type the commentators draw, that our offerings come to God by the hand of our great High Priest, who alone presents them and us to God, that they may be accepted; that we have access and acceptance only through the Mediator).

themes: thou shalt bring the meat offering... unto the LORD: and when it is presented unto the priest, he shall bring it unto the altar
the presenting of the offering through the priest
the meat offering brought to the LORD and presented through the priest to the altar
a type that our offerings come to God by our great High Priest, who alone presents them and us; access only through the Mediator

schools.summary: Gill: the priest took a memorial, an handful of it, of the fine flour or of the baked pieces, whether baked in the oven, pan, or frying pan, and burnt it; the witnesses note the priest taking a memorial of the meat offering, in whatever form, and burning it on the altar, a sweet savour to the LORD — the burning of the memorial-handful of the dressed offering too, well-pleasing (a memorial burnt to God, of every kind: 'the priest shall take from the meat offering a memorial thereof, and shall burn it upon the altar... of a sweet savour unto the LORD'; the memorial, 'a memorial thereof,' an handful, whether of the fine flour or of the baked pieces, in whatever form the offering was; the burning, 'burn it upon the altar,' the part offered to God; the acceptance, 'an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD,' well-pleasing and accepted; that the same memorial and the same sweet savour rose from the offering, however it was dressed; the type before given, that the sweet savour of Christ's graces and devotion ascended to God, a memorial accepted; that what is offered to God in faith is a sweet savour, in whatever form).

themes: the priest shall take from the meat offering a memorial thereof, and shall burn it upon the altar
of a sweet savour unto the LORD
the priest burning a memorial-handful of the meat offering, in whatever form, a sweet savour
the same memorial and sweet savour rising however dressed; the sweet savour of Christ's graces and devotion ascending, accepted

schools.summary: Gill: that which is left of the meat offering, not burnt with fire, was Aaron's and his sons'; the witnesses note God giving the remnant of the dressed meat offering, after the memorial was burnt, to Aaron and his sons, a thing most holy — the priests' portion of the dressed offering too, God's provision repeated (the rest the priests', and it most holy: 'that which is left of the meat offering shall be Aaron's and his sons': it is a thing most holy'; the portion, 'that which is left... not burnt with fire,' the part not offered to God, given to the priests; the holiness, 'a thing most holy,' that this, like the remnant of the raw offering, was most holy, the priests' food in the holy place; that the same provision was made of every form of the offering, the memorial to God and the rest to his priests; the meaning before given, that God provides for those who serve at his altar, and that they who feed on Christ, the meat offering, must be the consecrated; that God's ministers are fed from the holy things).

themes: And that which is left of the meat offering shall be Aaron's and his sons'
it is a thing most holy of the offerings of the LORD made by fire
the remnant of the dressed meat offering given to the priests, a thing most holy
God's provision repeated for every form, the memorial to God and the rest to his priests; God's ministers fed from the holy things

schools.summary: Gill: leaven might be used in peace offerings and in the wave loaves, but not in meat offerings, neither in the handful that was burnt nor in the rest; nor any honey burnt on the altar; the witnesses note God forbidding leaven in the meat offering and the burning of any leaven or honey in the offerings made by fire — the excluding of leaven and honey, the type of sin and of mere natural sweetness (nothing fermenting or corrupting going to the altar: 'No meat offering... shall be made with leaven: for ye shall burn no leaven, nor any honey, in any offering of the LORD made by fire'; the leaven forbidden, 'no meat offering... shall be made with leaven,' that leaven, which ferments and sours, was kept out, neither in the handful nor in the rest; the honey forbidden, 'nor any honey... burnt,' that honey, which ferments and turns sour, was also kept from the altar; the meaning the commentators draw, that leaven is the type of sin and corruption, the leaven of malice and wickedness, and honey of mere natural and fleshly sweetness, which puffs and ferments, both kept from the offering of Christ, who was without sin; that the offering of God, and Christ the offering, must be free from the leaven of sin and the honey of carnal sweetness; that our service too must be sincere and uncorrupt).

debates.question: Why were leaven and honey forbidden in the offerings made by fire?

sides.position: Because leaven is the type of sin and corruption (the leaven of malice and wickedness, 1 Corinthians 5:8) and honey of mere natural and fleshly sweetness, which ferments and puffs — both kept from the offering of Christ, who was without sin, and from our service, which must be sincere and uncorrupt

themes: No meat offering, which ye shall bring unto the LORD, shall be made with leaven
for ye shall burn no leaven, nor any honey, in any offering of the LORD made by fire
God forbidding leaven and honey in the offerings made by fire
leaven the type of sin, honey of carnal sweetness; both kept from the offering of Christ, who was without sin, and from our service

schools.summary: Gill: along with the oblation of the firstfruits, leaven and honey might be offered, but these were not to be burnt on the altar for a sweet savour; the witnesses note God appointing the oblation of firstfruits, with leaven and honey, to be offered to the LORD, but not burnt on the altar for a sweet savour — the distinction between what was given to God and what was burnt on the altar (leaven and honey might be brought, but not laid on the fire: 'the oblation of the firstfruits, ye shall offer them unto the LORD: but they shall not be burnt on the altar for a sweet savour'; the offering, 'the oblation of the firstfruits, ye shall offer them,' that the firstfruits, even with leaven and honey, might be brought and given to God, and were the priests' portion; the limit, 'they shall not be burnt on the altar for a sweet savour,' that, though offered, they were not to be laid on the altar-fire, the leaven and honey being kept from the burning; the distinction the commentators draw, that there is a difference between what God's people may give and enjoy and what is fit to be a sweet savour on the altar, the leaven and honey of nature being given but not burnt; that the things of nature may be sanctified and used, but only what is pure and uncorrupt rises as a sweet savour to God).

themes: As for the oblation of the firstfruits, ye shall offer them unto the LORD
but they shall not be burnt on the altar for a sweet savour
the firstfruits (with leaven and honey) offered to the LORD but not burnt on the altar
the distinction between what may be given and enjoyed and what is fit to be a sweet savour; only the pure and uncorrupt rises as a sweet savour to God

schools.summary: Gill: salt makes food savoury and preserves from putrefaction, denoting the savouriness and acceptableness of Christ as a meat offering to his people, and the salt of the covenant was never to be lacking; the witnesses note God commanding every meat offering to be seasoned with salt, the salt of the covenant of God, never lacking, with all offerings salt to be offered — the salt of the covenant, the savour and incorruption of grace, the perpetuity of the covenant (all the offerings seasoned with salt: 'every oblation of thy meat offering shalt thou season with salt; neither shalt thou suffer the salt of the covenant of thy God to be lacking... with all thine offerings thou shalt offer salt'; the command, 'season with salt,' that salt makes food savoury and preserves from putrefaction, and so was put on every offering; the meaning, denoting the savouriness and acceptableness of Christ as a meat offering to his people, and the grace that seasons and preserves; the great phrase, 'the salt of the covenant of thy God,' that salt, which preserves, was the token of the perpetual, incorruptible covenant, a covenant of salt for ever; the universality, 'with all thine offerings thou shalt offer salt,' that no offering was to lack it; the lessons the commentators draw, that grace must season all our service, let your speech be alway with grace, seasoned with salt, preserving it from the corruption of sin and hypocrisy, and that the covenant of God is sure and everlasting; that what we offer God must be savoury with grace and free from corruption).

debates.question: What does the salt of the covenant, put on every offering, signify?

sides.position: The savour and incorruption of grace — salt being savoury and preserving from putrefaction (denoting Christ savoury to his people, and the grace that must season all our service, Colossians 4:6) — and the token of the perpetual, incorruptible covenant of God ('a covenant of salt for ever,' Numbers 18:19), free from the corruption of sin and hypocrisy

themes: every oblation of thy meat offering shalt thou season with salt; neither shalt thou suffer the salt of the covenant of thy God to be lacking
with all thine offerings thou shalt offer salt
every offering seasoned with the salt of the covenant, never to be lacking
salt the savour and incorruption of grace, the token of the perpetual covenant; grace must season all our service, free from corruption
